When Marcus saw what was taking place, he viewed it as an excellent opportunity. He immediately dispatched his light-armed troops, with orders to engage the enemy and contest the possession of the position. After these, he sent his cavalry, followed closely by his heavy-armed troops in person, as on a previous occasion, intending to repeat the same tactics. 105

To those who were actually engaged, it was clear that Minucius's rashness had led to an utter defeat. Their safety on this and previous occasions had been secured by Fabius's caution. Those at home had a clear demonstration of the difference between the rashness of a soldier and the prudence of a general. The Romans, following Fabius' advice, joined forces once again. The Carthaginians responded by digging a trench across the space between the knoll and their own lines, then constructing a palisade around the captured hill's crest. With a guard in place, they completed their preparations for winter without interruption. 106 Aemulus Paulus On their appointment, the Dictators laid down their offices. The Consuls of the previous year, Gnaeus Servilius and Marcus Regulus, who had been appointed after the death of Flaminius, were invested with proconsular authority by Aemilius. Taking command at the seat of war, they administered the affairs of the army independently.

The details of this part of the campaign are not worth recording. Nothing of significance or value took place, largely due to the restrictive instructions and unforeseen circumstances. However, there were numerous skirmishes and minor battles, during which the Roman commanders earned a notable reputation for bravery and sound judgment. 107 They therefore sent frequent messages to Rome seeking guidance: for if they approached the enemy, they would not be able to avoid an engagement, given that the country was being plundered and their allies were in a state of excitement. The Senate ordered a battle. The Senate passed a resolution that they should give the enemy battle. However, they instructed Gnaeus Servilius to wait, and dispatched the consuls to the seat of war.

The Romans, as previously stated, habitually enroll four legions annually. Each legion consists of approximately 4,000 infantrymen and 200 cavalrymen. In cases where an extraordinary need arises, they increase the infantry contingent to 5,000 men and the cavalry to 300. To Aemilius,

With earnest words of exhortation, I put before you the gravity of the situation from every angle. As your consuls, we are now heading to the battlefield.

(Note: The original text was likely a fragment or a quote, and it appears to be from ancient Roman history. The corrected text aims to preserve the original meaning while making it more coherent and readable.) They dispatched him with instructions to seek a favorable opportunity to fight a decisive battle with courage worthy of Rome. Those who had been defeated at Trebia were assembled on the field at daybreak, the morning after their arrival from Sicily. Those who had fought in Etruria had never seen the enemy before, nor did they encounter them during the battle itself, due to the unfavorable conditions of the atmosphere. 109

The men had not only seen the enemy's arms, order, and numbers, but had been engaged in almost daily fights with them for the last two years. The conditions under which the two former battles were fought were quite different. It was therefore natural that the result of the coming struggle would also be different. Do not frustrate those hopes: pay back to your country the gratitude you owe it. Make it clear to all the world that the former reverses occurred, not because the Romans are worse men than the Carthaginians, but from the lack of experience on the part of those who were fighting, and through a combination of adverse circumstances. Aemilius dismissed the troops. 110

When Aemilius observed that the ground was flat and bare for some distance around, he said that they should not engage the enemy there, given their superior cavalry; instead, they should try to lure him off and lead him to a battlefield where the infantry could gain the upper hand. Hannibal set his light-armed troops and cavalry into motion to meet the Romans. As he charged, the Romans were still on the march when Hannibal attacked them. They took him by surprise and caused great confusion among his troops. The Romans repulsed the first charge by deploying some of their heavy-armed troops in front; then they sent forward their light-armed and cavalry units, which began to gain the upper hand along the entire line. The Carthaginians had no significant reserves, while certain companies of legionaries were integrated with the Roman light-armed, helping to sustain the battle.

He instructed them to survey the land and asked, "Is it not a fortunate circumstance that they were granted this opportunity to engage in battle on the very terrain they encountered, given the significant advantage of cavalry at their disposal?" And when all had signified their acquiescence in such an evident truth, he added: "First, then, give thanks to the gods; for they have brought the enemy into our country, as was their design for our victory." You now possess the country and all its wealth, as promised. I have been entirely truthful in every statement I've made to you. Hannibal annoyed his enemies. He drew out his men along the riverbank the next day and was eager to engage the enemy in battle. Hannibal waited for a considerable time, expecting an attack, but none came. With no one in sight, he ordered the remaining army to set up camp once more. However, he dispatched his Numidian cavalry to launch a surprise attack on the enemy's water supply units from the smaller camp.

The Roman horse was stationed on the right wing along the river. Their foot soldiers were positioned next to them in the same line, with the maniples placed closer together than usual. This resulted in a deeper formation for each maniple, making it several times deeper than its front edge. The Roman sword could thrust with as deadly an effect as it could cut, whereas the Gallic sword could only cut. This required some space. As the Roman army faced the south and the Carthaginian forces faced the north, the rays of the rising sun posed no threat to either side. 115

When the Carthaginians gained the upper hand in this encounter, they killed most of their opponents on the ground. The Romans, however, continued to fight with spirit and determination, forcing the remaining opponents to flee along the river. As they advanced, the soldiers gave no quarter, and then the legionaries took their place, closing in on the enemy. The Roman legions followed with spirit, and easily cut their way through the enemy's line. The Celts, having formed a narrow line, were quickly overwhelmed as the Romans closed in from the wings to converge on the center and the point of danger. For the two wings did not come into action at the same time as the centre; however, the centre was first engaged. This was because the Gauls, having been stationed on the arc of a crescent, had come into contact with the enemy long before the wings arrived, as the convex side of the crescent faced the enemy.

The Romans, pursuing the troops, closed in quickly on the center and the part of the enemy that was retreating. They advanced far enough for the Libyan heavy-armed troops on both wings to be on their flanks. Those on the right, facing to the left, charged from the right upon the Roman flank. Those who were on the left wing faced to the right and, dressed on the left, charged their right flank. The urgency of the situation prompted them to take action. The Numidian horse on the Carthaginian right were charging the cavalry on the Roman left. Despite their unique fighting style, which made them less vulnerable to damage, they still managed to render the enemy's horses useless by keeping them occupied and attacking them from different sides. When Hasdrubal arrived after having nearly destroyed the cavalry by the river, he led a new charge from the left to support the Numidians. The Roman allied cavalry, anticipating his approach, panicked and fled in disarray.

Seeing the Numidians as strong in numbers, and more effective and formidable against troops that had once been forced from their ground, he left the pursuit to them. He himself hastened to the part of the field where the infantry were engaged and brought his men up to support the Libyans. He then charged the Roman legions from behind, harassing them with a series of squadrons launched simultaneously across multiple fronts. This boosted the morale of the Libyans, while demoralizing and disheartening the Romans. While this struggle and carnage were underway, the Numidian horse pursued the fugitives, cutting down most of them or hurling them from their horses. A few escaped into Venusia, among whom was Gaius Terentius, the Consul, who sought a flight as disgraceful to himself as his conduct in office had been disastrous to his country. 117

Lucius Aemilius left 10,000 infantry in his camp. If Hannibal were to disregard the safety of his own camp and take his entire army into battle, they could seize an opportunity while the fight was ongoing to force their way in and capture the enemy's baggage. Alternatively, if Hannibal were to leave a guard in his camp, the number of enemy troops on the field would be reduced. The outcome of this battle, as I have described it, had the consequences that both sides had anticipated.

The Romans, after this disaster, lost hope of maintaining their dominance over the Italians. They were in a state of utmost panic, fearing for their very lives and the survival of their city. Every moment they expected Hannibal's arrival.  106 For as if Fortune herself were allied with the disasters that had already befallen them, it happened that only a few days later, while the city was still in this state of panic, the Praetor who had been sent to Gaul fell into an ambush and perished. His army was subsequently annihilated by the Celts. The Senate left no means untried to save the state.

I shall, therefore, conclude this book here, having now recounted the events in Iberia and Italy. C 216 The 140th Olympic Games. When I have arrived at the same period in my history of Greece during this Olympiad, I shall then fulfill my promise of devoting a book to a formal account of the Roman constitution. I believe that a description of it will not only be relevant to the matter of my history but also be of great help to practical statesmen and students, whether they are reforming or establishing other constitutions. I shall now take up the history of Greece during the same period, ending at the same date, and commence from the 140th Olympiad. C  , cc 41-71 There was a democratic federal government in place. The league was subsequently dissolved by the kings of Sparta, who divided it among their separate towns and villages.

Philip, son of Demetrius, had just become king of Macedonia. Achaeus, prince of Asia Minor, had converted his display of power into a reality. Antiochus the Great had succeeded to the throne of Syria shortly after his brother Seleucus' death. Ariarathes had taken over Cappadocia, and Ptolemy Philopator had become king of Egypt. Lycurgus soon became king of Sparta, and the Carthaginians had recently elected Hannibal as their general to continue the war that was recently described.

Every government that had changed about this time seemed likely to introduce a new departure in policy, which is natural and usual. In fact, it did occur at this time. For the Romans and Carthaginians entered into the war I have described; Antiochus and Ptolemy on one side for the possession of Coele-Syria; and the Achaeans and Philip on the other against the Aetolians and Lacedaemonians. The causes of this last war must now be stated.

Chance played a role in giving them the opportunity they sought. A crowd of pirates flocked to him at Phigalea; and being unable to get them any booty by fair means, because the peace between all Greeks which Antigonus had concluded was still in force, he was finally reduced to allowing the pirates to drive off the cattle of the Messenians, though they were friends and allies of the Aetolians. The Messenians were naturally indignant and sent embassies to Dorimachus. He initially disregarded them, as he sought to benefit both his soldiers and himself by gaining a share of their spoils.

When embassies from such places arrived with increasing frequency, due to the repeated acts of plundering, he finally decided to visit Messene in person and settle the claims the Aetolians had against them. He arrived, but when the afflicted people appeared, he ridiculed some, warned others that he would strike them, and chased others away with harsh words. 4 While in Messene, the pirates approached the city walls at night and used scaling-ladders to break into a country house known as Chiron's villa. They killed any slaves who resisted and took the rest captive. After this, Dorimachus left Messene. They took the cattle away with them.

When therefore he was speaking in these threatening and insolent tones to the Messenian magistrates, Sciron lost his temper and said, "Do you think we care for you or your threats, Babyrtas?" After his return to Aetolia, Dorimachus nursed a bitter and furious feeling of anger at being taunted by the Messenians. He then launched a war against them, driven solely by this cause. 5 Dorimachus could not urge the Aetolians to undertake the Messenian war in his public capacity because he lacked a reasonable pretext. The origin of his wish was known to everyone: it stemmed from the wrongs he himself had committed and the bitter insult they had hurled at him. He therefore gave up the idea of publicly advocating the war. He induced Scopas to go to war with Messenia, Epirus, Achaia, Acarnania, and Macedonia. But Philip tried to induce Scopas privately to join in the intrigue against the Messenians. He pointed out that there was now no danger from the side of Macedonia, owing to the youth of the king (Philip being then only seventeen years old). The Lacedaemonians were alienated from the Messenians, and they possessed the affection and alliance of the Eleans. Taking these circumstances together would make an invasion of Messenia perfectly safe. The argument most truly Aetolian which he used was that a great booty could be obtained from Messenia, as it was entirely unguarded and had alone, among all the Peloponnesian districts, remained untouched throughout the Cleomenic War.

Timoxenus, the Achaean Strategus, with the assistance of Taurion, who had been left in charge of Macedonian interests in the Peloponnese by Antigonus, took over after a brief siege. For Antigonus retained Corinth, in accordance with his convention with the Achaeans, made at the time of the Cleomenic war. He had never restored Orchomenus to the Achaeans after he took it by force, but claimed and retained it for himself. His intention was likely to command the entrance of the Peloponnese while also securing its interior through his garrison and military equipment in Orchomenus. While marching through Patrae, Pharae, and Tritaea, they pretended not to wish to do any harm to the Achaeans. However, their forces, driven by an inveterate passion for plunder, could not be restrained from robbing the country. Consequently, they committed acts of violence and outrages along their entire route until they arrived at Phigalea.

When the assembly met, deputies from Patrae and Pharae made a formal statement about the injuries inflicted upon their territories during the passage of the Aetolians. An embassy from Messenia also appeared, begging for their assistance on the grounds that the treatment they were receiving was unjust and in defiance of treaty. Now Timoxenus, the existing Strategus, was just on the point of quitting office. He felt a lack of confidence in the Achaeans because martial exercise had fallen into neglect among them. Therefore, he hesitated to undertake the expedition or even summon the popular levy. The expulsion of Cleomenes, King of Sparta, led to a significant shift in the balance of power among the city-states of ancient Greece. The Peloponnesians, who had previously been allies with Cleomenes, now found themselves at odds with him. Their decision was likely driven by a desire to maintain their own influence and control over the region. C 222-221 We were weary of the wars that had taken place, and trusting in the peaceful arrangement that had been reached, we neglected all warlike preparations.

Aratus, however, was indignant and incensed by the Aetolians' audacity. He had a longstanding grudge against them. The Thessalian cavalry excels in both squadron and column formations. However, when their formation breaks up, they lack the skill to skirmish effectively, as each man relies solely on instinct rather than adapting to the situation. In contrast, the Aetolians possess a unique ability to thrive in chaotic conditions, where their individual initiative allows them to capitalize on opportunities that might otherwise be missed. I have made the following statement, so that my readers will give me credit when I have to repeat myself or contradict myself on the same individuals or in similar situations.

The Achaean magistrates declined the offered alliance, citing the impossibility of admitting a new member without Philip's and the other allies' concurrence. This was due to the sworn alliance negotiated by Antigonus during the Cleomenic war, which still bound Achaia, Epirus, Phocis, Macedonia, Boeotia, Acarnania, and Thessaly. However, they agreed to march out to their relief if the envoys present would place their sons in Sparta as hostages for their promise not to make terms with the Aetolians without Achaeans' consent. They were ready to follow orders. They therefore immediately sent dispatches to Cyllene and to the Aetolian Strategus, Ariston, requesting that transports be sent to a place on the coast of Elis called the island of Pheia. Two days later, they struck camp and, laden with spoils, marched towards Elis. When Dorimachus learned that Aratus was thus watching his march, he turned against Aratus. With a sufficient number of men to secure their passage, he sent his booty on to the transports under orders to meet him at Rhium, where he intended to embark. Meanwhile, he remained for a time to superintend and protect the shipment of the booty before changing the direction of his march and advancing towards Olympia.
